# 📝 Notes

Apr 8, 2026

## 8am AI\!

Invited [David Olsson](mailto:david@atomic47.co) [eric@scroll.ca](mailto:eric@scroll.ca) [giroday@gmail.com](mailto:giroday@gmail.com) [hanif.janmohamed@gmail.com](mailto:hanif.janmohamed@gmail.com) [Fulvio Ciano](mailto:fulvio@atomic47.co) [scott@driftresourcetechnologies.com](mailto:scott@driftresourcetechnologies.com) [travis@atomic47.co](mailto:travis@atomic47.co) [Jason Kryski](mailto:jason@mttr.ca) [Jen Boger](mailto:jen@atomic47.co) [Shabraj Shahid](mailto:shabraj@mttr.ca) [spencer@ratiomachina.com](mailto:spencer@ratiomachina.com) [Ashley Beckett](mailto:ashley@mttr.ca) [graham@ratiomachina.com](mailto:graham@ratiomachina.com) [Bailey Klinger](mailto:bwklinger@gmail.com) [andre.bertram3@gmail.com](mailto:andre.bertram3@gmail.com)

Attachments [8am AI\!](https://calendar.google.com/calendar/event?eid=aW52N2w4MWJvNjJqYm5zYmQxZTMzYjllNmZfMjAyNjA0MDhUMTUwMDAwWiBkYXZpZEBhdG9taWM0Ny5jbw)

Meeting records [Transcript](https://docs.google.com/document/d/16XB5L2Ww59R6JRGyvQeyBJb7QN46Iea29Vyn0AwnCNY/edit?usp=drive_web&tab=t.8u5emgjur1wk) 

### Summary

Meeting discussions centered on AI integration challenges and the necessity of architectural skills for developers.

**Developer Role and Skillset**  
Participants identified a critical shift where developers must evolve into architects to effectively guide AI tools. Developers rely too heavily on automation, losing the foundational knowledge required for debugging.

**AI Integration and Strategy**  
Teams are transitioning from standard status reports to strategic planning sessions. Automation of strategic storytelling and codebase analysis helps maintain business focus in a rapidly changing market.

**Establishing First Principles**  
Development teams successfully implemented a framework of 16 design-thinking based first principles to ensure projects remain constitutionally sound. Quality output now depends on defining these principles before execution.

### Next steps

- [ ] \[Jason Kryski\] Research Revolutions: Review technological revolutions, analyze time between introduction and integration. Figure out adoption stages for grounding.

- [ ] \[Jason Kryski\] Share Paper: Distribute the produced paper detailing physics-based equation for deadheading attention paths in transformers.

- [ ] \[David Olsson\] Send Email: Draft and send follow-up email regarding discussion topics.

- [ ] \[David Olsson\] Communicate Group: Reach out to individuals about the new group focused on the build experience.

### Details

*Did the screenshots in this section make your notes [better](https://goo.gle/44IH7J9) or [worse](https://goo.gle/4b5ZWtJ)?*

* **Initial Business Context and Growth Challenges**: David Olsson described their current process of growing the company by attempting to merge with another organization, which involves a complex orchestration of many concurrent activities, reflecting the complexities of modern software development ([00:00:00](#00:00:00)). They observed that the efficiency experienced in one individual's workflow does not always translate across an entire organization, leading to a concurrent learning environment where everybody is adjusting ([00:01:38](#00:01:38)). This environment makes collaboration difficult, and David Olsson expressed interest in sharing tools they built to promote better sharing and collaboration within teams ([00:02:59](#00:02:59)).

* **Challenges in Developer Collaboration and Upskilling**: Juan Rojas identified people as a major blocker, particularly in getting team members up to speed and thinking differently about new tools ([00:04:21](#00:04:21)). They explained that developers struggle to grasp the value of having a "meta repository" and the complexity of retrieving information, often lacking the experience to know what questions to ask when troubleshooting ([00:05:40](#00:05:40)). This lack of understanding of underlying concepts like asynchronous queues means they cannot identify where issues are broken within microservices ([00:06:48](#00:06:48)).

* **Developer Dependency on LLMs and Lack of Comprehension**: Juan Rojas noted that developers often rely heavily on Large Language Models (LLMs) and lack comprehension, sometimes just pasting a Jira ticket and asking for a solution ([00:06:48](#00:06:48)). They shared an example of a team member who likely did not build a solution for three months but instead relied on prompting and committing without testing, indicating a dependence on "magic" rather than understanding ([00:07:58](#00:07:58)).

* **Value of the Code Repository as a Knowledge Base**: David Olsson affirmed Juan Rojas's point, recognizing the power of the repository as a knowledge base for "meta work" and having a single understanding of many things ([00:07:58](#00:07:58)). They noted that a standard wiki format, due to the LLMs, can be incredibly valuable compared to RAG because it already possesses a lot of structure ([00:09:10](#00:09:10)).

* **Mentoring Approach to Problem Solving**: Juan Rojas confirmed that they are actively walking their team through problem-solving steps, explaining the "why" behind the prompts and questions to improve their understanding of debugging and platform issues. They stressed that while new feature development is easier with LLMs, troubleshooting is where developers struggle, as they lack the awareness needed to ask better questions ([00:09:10](#00:09:10)).

* **Shift from Coders to Architects in the Age of AI**: Fulvio Ciano argued that as AI develops, there is less need for coders and more need for architects who understand how to steer the code ([00:10:16](#00:10:16)). They proposed that developers must understand the project's corpus, as code writing may soon be automated, making the shared vision and project context the main point of interaction ([00:11:31](#00:11:31)). Fulvio Ciano suggested that "coders" will need to become developers with architectural skills, understanding the legacy knowledge of lower-level systems to properly direct the AI ([00:12:53](#00:12:53)).

* **The Importance of Foundational Knowledge**: Juan Rojas agreed, emphasizing the need to be able to "combat" AI suggestions and critique an approach. They, along with David Olsson and Fulvio Ciano, highlighted that developers who did not learn foundational layers—such as how assembly or memory works—lack the context to control AI outputs or understand why an answer is not good ([00:14:10](#00:14:10)).

* **Concerns about Learning and Skill Gaps**: David Olsson drew a parallel to their daughter using tools like Google Lens to cheat on exams, which circumvents learning critical thinking skills ([00:16:02](#00:16:02)). Fulvio Ciano related this to the premise of the cartoon \*Wall-E\*, suggesting that humans are relying so heavily on machines they are losing foundational skills across various domains ([00:17:04](#00:17:04)).

* **The Focus on End Goals Over Process**: David Olsson observed that people, including their daughter, are focusing on the end goal and not the process, which they consider "cheating on your homework" ([00:18:10](#00:18:10)). They questioned whether taking shortcuts allows for the development of deeper cognitive skills or thematic understanding, suggesting that the goal of the teacher should shift to getting students so engaged they have no desire to cheat ([00:18:58](#00:18:58)).

* **The Changing Nature of Development and Team Dynamics**: Jason Kryski asked if working in teams is harder now than before the shift, noting that he struggles to find a logical development cycle and product role because the established loop is fundamentally broken ([00:22:05](#00:22:05)). They described an environment where they, the "pirate," build what they want and hand it off to an architect for integration, questioning the necessity of traditional product roles and the optimal size and skillset of a functional team ([00:24:46](#00:24:46)).

* **The Challenge of Slow Processes and Shifting Standups**: Juan Rojas confirmed that things "feel slow" compared to current capabilities and is attempting to change team processes. They are transforming daily standups from status reports into strategy and planning sessions, requiring team members to bring a plan or strategy for a product ticket to foster better discussion and tactical thinking ([00:25:53](#00:25:53)).

* **Contextual Learning and AI Application**: Juan Rojas shared an insight from their children using AI for game development in Roblox Studio and Minecraft: success depends on knowing the rules of the world, allowing them to prompt correctly despite having no code knowledge ([00:26:56](#00:26:56)). David Olsson agreed that this "comfort zone" of context is crucial for successful AI integration ([00:28:01](#00:28:01)).

* **The Development of Reports for Grounded Intelligence**: David Olsson demonstrated a toolkit that runs 48 reports against a codebase to produce value- and understanding-oriented reports for storytelling, onboarding, and determining product costs at scale ([00:30:23](#00:30:23)). They stated that the essential skill moving forward is the ability to read quickly and synthesize information, supported by deep critical thinking and articulation ([00:31:24](#00:31:24)).

  ![][image1]

* **Automating Strategic Storytelling and Decision-Making**: David Olsson used the generated reports as a knowledge base, feeding them into Claude to perform strategic work like creating dashboards and suggesting a strategic roadmap, which acts as a form of "wild storytelling" for the codebase ([00:33:12](#00:33:12)). They proposed that AI can create better stories with grounded intelligence, arguing that the product owner and manager roles might become automated, consolidating market, UX, and KPI understanding from the codebase up ([00:35:07](#00:35:07)).

* **Existential Crisis and the Pace of Technological Change**: Jason Kryski supported David Olsson's insights, noting the difficulty in keeping up with methodical research in development flows ([00:37:24](#00:37:24)). They expressed an "existential crisis" due to the inability to forecast the market when technology is changing so rapidly, citing a recent finding where a physics equation mapped perfectly to pruning dead attention heads in transformers ([00:38:21](#00:38:21)).

* **The Need for Business Focus in a Changing Market**: David Olsson and Jason Kryski discussed the dilemma of what to build and why, with David Olsson noting that their company has shifted focus to projects where applying current technology creates unique opportunities ([00:41:54](#00:41:54)). David Olsson observed that the lengthening of a sales lead increases the likelihood that the client will "vibe it themselves," prompting the adoption of risk-sharing models with clients ([00:42:48](#00:42:48)).

* **The Importance of Tacit Knowledge and Automation**: David Olsson stressed that knowledge about industry adoption curves and market dynamics is IP that is being lost as companies reduce personnel ([00:44:43](#00:44:43)). Jason Kryski noted that the biggest transformation issue is that most businesses don't know how AI can help ([00:45:51](#00:45:51)). They discussed the concept of automating the consultancy and interview process, potentially using voice models to gather prior context, which David Olsson described as the one piece they want to automate ([00:47:00](#00:47:00)).

* **Knowledge Bases and Strategic Learning**: David Olsson emphasized that creating a knowledge base from a corpus transforms information into something useful, though handing off 500 pages of documentation is ineffective ([00:47:54](#00:47:54)). They stated their positive strategy involves building reports that are "curious" and challenging, aimed at providing new insights ([00:50:44](#00:50:44)).

* **The Paradigm Shift and Existential Disorientation**: Hanif Janmohamed characterized the current period as running through several paradigm shifts simultaneously, leading to significant disorientation. They cited the Palantir CEO's idea that "the middle is dead," advocating for tradespeople and neurodivergent individuals, which points to a fundamental shift in value ([00:51:51](#00:51:51)).

* **Personal Transformation and Future Questions**: Juan Rojas expressed satisfaction that the current tools are "awesome" and that they are successfully building things to replace their role as the sole solution provider and planner ([00:55:48](#00:55:48)). The next questions for them involve managing the generation of code with better LLMs at the enterprise level, specifically how to use prompts to create new things that follow necessary patterns and ensure scaling ([00:57:07](#00:57:07)).

* **The Continued Need for Human Critical Thinking in the Age of AI**: Juan expressed the belief that human critical thinking, experience, and common sense remain highly needed, noting that AI can sometimes lead people to believe that complex tasks are too easy. They cited conversations with executives who attempt to bypass coding simple tasks, cautioning that applying business logic and transactional requirements is likely not as simple as a single prompt. David Olsson agreed that it is a qualitative matter, stating that the quality of the prompt is more important than the prompt itself ([00:58:32](#00:58:32)).

* **Application of Design Principles and Qualitative Analysis in Code**: David Olsson described their involvement in a qualitative project and their work to define quality, which they acknowledged is subjective. They used AI to analyze their code and build a set of 16 "first principles," primarily design-thinking based, that they can now apply to documentation, specifications, or codebases ([00:59:38](#00:59:38)). These principles were applied to Hanif Janmohamed's project, Stone Maps, by tracing the constitution's philosophical tendencies through the code, the chosen codebase, libraries, and user experience ([01:00:31](#01:00:31)).

* **Qualifying Constitutional Soundness in Project Development**: David Olsson explained that they were able to set up principles, requirements, and documentation ahead of time, using them to produce a report for Hanif Janmohamed. This report demonstrated how the project's code could be qualified as "constitutionally sound" based on the established principles and documentation ([01:00:31](#01:00:31)). Hanif Janmohamed affirmed that the process of translating a soft corpus (the constitution) into hard code and integrating it was "pretty remarkable" ([01:01:30](#01:01:30)).

* **Advanced AI Model Capabilities and Future Communication**: David Olsson mentioned using the Opus 46 model, noting that the business reports it produces are significantly better. David Olsson stated they would send a follow-up email about making processes easier and plans to communicate with some participants about a separate, more focused group concerning the "build experience". They concluded the current session by thanking the participants and wishing them a good day ([01:01:30](#01:01:30)).

*You should review Gemini's notes to make sure they're accurate. [Get tips and learn how Gemini takes notes](https://support.google.com/meet/answer/14754931)*
